verses.commentary: Feast of Unleavened Bread — called Passover — drew near. Passion week now framed by Israel's chief feast of liberation.
Chief priests and scribes were seeking how to put him to death — for they feared the people. Crowd-fear still constraining.
Satan entered into Judas, called Iscariot, one of the Twelve. Lukan attribution: betrayal as demonic incursion.
He went to the chief priests and officers of the temple guard — discussed how to betray Jesus. The conspiracy gains an insider.
They were glad, agreed to give him money. Convenient solution to their problem.
He consented — sought opportunity to deliver him over in the absence of a crowd. Operational details negotiated.
Day of Unleavened Bread came — when the Passover lamb had to be sacrificed. Sacrificial framing operative.
Jesus sent Peter and John: 'Go, prepare the Passover for us that we may eat it.' Lukan precision — names the two.
'Where will you have us prepare?'
'As you enter the city — a man carrying a jar of water (unusual — water-carrying was typically women's work) will meet you. Follow him into the house he enters.'
Say to the master: 'The Teacher asks, Where is the guest room where I may eat the Passover with my disciples?'
He will show you a large upper room furnished — prepare it there. Either pre-arranged or supernaturally foreseen.
They went, found it as he had told them — prepared the Passover. Compliance and confirmation.
When the hour came — he reclined at the table with the apostles. The hour (hora, ὥρα) is the eschatological moment.
'With desire I have desired (epithymia epethymesa, ἐπιθυμίᾳ ἐπεθύμησα — Hebraic intensifier) to eat this Passover with you before I suffer.' Strong longing expressed.
'I will not eat it until it is fulfilled (plerothe, πληρωθῇ) in the kingdom of God.' Eschatological Passover anticipated.
Took a cup — gave thanks — 'Take this and divide it among yourselves.' Lukan unique — a pre-bread cup, possibly the second of four Passover cups.
'I will not drink of the fruit of the vine until the kingdom of God comes.' Vow of abstention until consummation.

Took bread — gave thanks — broke it — gave to them: 'This is my body, given for you (didomenon hyper hymon, διδόμενον ὑπὲρ ὑμῶν). Do this in remembrance of me (eis ten emen anamnesin, εἰς τὴν ἐμὴν ἀνάμνησιν).' Lukan 'remembrance' phrase — institutes ongoing observance.

Cup after supper: 'This cup is the new covenant (kaine diatheke, καινὴ διαθήκη) in my blood, which is poured out for you.' Jeremiah 31:31 fulfilled — new covenant ratified in blood. 'For you' personal.
'Behold, the hand of him who betrays me is with mine on the table.' Communion meal with betrayer present.
'The Son of Man goes as it has been determined — but woe to that man by whom he is betrayed.' Sovereignty and culpability both.
They began to question among themselves which of them it could be — which would do this. Self-inquiry.
A dispute arose among them — which of them was to be regarded greatest. Lukan irony: at the Last Supper, status-competition.
Kings of the Gentiles lord it over them — those in authority over them are called Benefactors (Euergetai, Εὐεργέται — actual title used by Hellenistic kings).
'Not so among you — let the greatest among you become as the youngest, the leader as one who serves.' Hierarchical inversion.
Who is greater — the one reclining or the one serving? Is not the one reclining? Yet I am among you as the one who serves. Christ as table-server. The model is enacted (compare John 13 footwashing).
'You are those who have stayed with me in my trials (peirasmois, πειρασμοῖς).' Loyalty in trial honored.
I assign to you (diatithemai, διατίθεμαι — bequeath by covenant), as my Father assigned to me, a kingdom.
That you may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom and sit on thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel. Eschatological co-regency.
'Simon, Simon — behold, Satan has demanded to sift (siniasai, σινιάσαι) all of you (plural) like wheat.' Cosmic context: spiritual warfare around the Twelve.

'But I have prayed for you (Peter, singular) that your faith may not fail — when you have turned again, strengthen your brothers.' Peter's denial AND restoration both foreseen. Restoration assumed before the fall is reported. Lukan unique tenderness.
Peter: 'Lord, I am ready to go with you both to prison and to death.' Overconfidence.
'I tell you, Peter — the rooster will not crow this day until you deny three times that you know me.' Precise prediction.
'When I sent you without moneybag, knapsack, or sandals — did you lack anything?' 'Nothing.'

'Now let the one who has a moneybag take it — the one with a knapsack also. The one who has no sword — sell his cloak and buy one.' Lukan unique — ministry context changes; previously protected, now exposed.
'It is written: He was numbered with the transgressors (Isaiah 53:12). This must be fulfilled in me. What is written about me has its fulfillment.' Servant-Christology explicit.
'Look, Lord, here are two swords.' 'It is enough.' Likely irony — the disciples' literal misreading is dismissed, not endorsed.
Went out as customary to the Mount of Olives — disciples followed. Familiar pattern; familiar place.
On arrival: 'Pray that you may not enter into temptation (peirasmon, πειρασμόν).' The same word as in Lord's Prayer.
Withdrew about a stone's throw (lithou bolen, λίθου βολήν), knelt down and prayed.
'Father, if you are willing, remove this cup from me. Nevertheless, not my will, but yours, be done.' Submission. Lukan version retains the conditional and submission both.
An angel from heaven appeared, strengthening him. (Verses 43-44 are textually disputed — present in some early manuscripts, absent in others. Most modern translations retain with footnote.)

In agony he prayed more earnestly — his sweat became like great drops of blood falling to the ground (thromboi haimatos, θρόμβοι αἵματος — clots of blood). Possibly hematidrosis — a real medical phenomenon under extreme stress. The agony is physical and spiritual.
Rose from prayer — came to disciples — found them sleeping for sorrow (apo tes lypes, ἀπὸ τῆς λύπης). Lukan compassion-explanation: grief, not laziness, exhausted them.
'Why are you sleeping? Rise and pray that you may not enter temptation.' Same word again — peirasmos.
While he was still speaking — a crowd. Judas, one of the Twelve, leading them. He drew near to kiss Jesus.
'Judas, would you betray the Son of Man with a kiss?' Tender naming. The betrayal-instrument named.
Those around him: 'Lord, shall we strike with the sword?'
One of them struck the servant of the high priest, cut off his right ear. Hand-of-disciple violence.
'No more of this!' Touched his ear, healed him. Lukan unique — the slave is healed. The last miracle before the cross is mercy to an opponent.
To chief priests, officers, elders who had come out against him: 'Have you come out as against a robber, with swords and clubs?'

'When I was with you day after day in the temple, you did not lay hands on me. But this is your hour, and the power of darkness (he exousia tou skotous, ἡ ἐξουσία τοῦ σκότους).' The night and its principality named.
Seizing him, led him away, brought him into the high priest's house. Peter followed at a distance (apo makrothen, ἀπὸ μακρόθεν).
Kindled a fire in the middle of the courtyard, sat down together. Peter sat with them.
A servant girl seeing him as he sat in the light — looked closely (atenisasa, ἀτενίσασα) — 'This man also was with him.'
He denied: 'Woman, I do not know him.' First denial.
A little later, another saw him: 'You also are one of them.' Peter: 'Man, I am not.' Second denial.
After about an hour, another insisted: 'Certainly this man also was with him — he too is a Galilean.' Accent reveals identity.
Peter: 'Man, I do not know what you are talking about.' Third denial. Immediately, while he was still speaking — the rooster crowed.

The Lord turned and looked at Peter (Lukan unique — the gaze across the courtyard). Peter remembered the word: 'Before the rooster crows today, you will deny me three times.' Recognition by silent look.
Went out and wept bitterly (eklausen pikros, ἔκλαυσεν πικρῶς). Repentance begins in tears.
The men holding Jesus mocked and beat him.
Blindfolded him, asked: 'Prophesy! Who struck you?' Mocking his prophetic claim.
And they said many other blasphemies (blasphemountes, βλασφημοῦντες) against him. The irony — blaspheming the holy one whom they accuse of blasphemy.
When day came — assembly of elders, chief priests, scribes — led him to their council (synedrion, συνέδριον).
'If you are the Christ, tell us.' 'If I tell you, you will not believe.'
'If I ask you, you will not answer.' The dialogue is already-decided; honest exchange impossible.
'From now on the Son of Man shall be seated at the right hand of the power of God.' Psalm 110:1 echo. Vindication declared even before trial completes.
All said: 'Are you the Son of God, then?' 'You say that I am.' Confirmation that they have correctly inferred his claim.
'What further testimony do we need? We have heard it ourselves from his own lips.' Verdict assumed without deliberation.
